# CLAUDE INSIGHTS

# ***SYDE 361 — Campus Climate Monitoring Tool***

## ***Design Synthesis, Insights, Features, Prototype Recommendation & Project Plan***

*Synthesized from seven independent Needs & Desires assessments | June 26, 2026*

---

# ***PART 1 — Research Synthesis***

## ***Cross-Interview Synthesis Methodology***

*Across the seven documents, fourteen distinct human participants were interviewed (plus six more-than-human subjects). Rather than treating each document as a self-contained report, the synthesis below looks for patterns that recurred across multiple independent interviews, contradictions worth naming, and insights that only become visible when the data is read together.*

*The participants (human, named or coded) break down as:*

***SYDE engineering students:** Aksha (Syra's doc), Yashika (Pahul's doc), Yashvanth (Jenifer's and Ishan's docs — appears in both), Syra Singh (Aksha's doc) **Non-SYDE students:** Sona Singh (Syra's doc, Arts & Business), Anandi Jawkar (Pahul's doc, CS & BBA), Alex (Jenifer's doc, Science & Business), Kaven Thamilventhan (Ishan's doc, Mech Eng / Varsity Soccer), Pereenn (Aksha's doc, Psychology) **More-than-human beings interviewed:** Canada Goose at Waterloo Park (Syra), Silver Lake (Pahul), Laurel Creek (Yashika), a Rose Bush at Ken's Garden (Jenifer and Aksha — independently, same location), a Daylily at QNC garden (Ishan)*

---

## ***Research Synthesis Table***

| Theme | Supporting Participants | Representative Evidence | Frequency | Design Significance |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| T1. Standard weather apps are the wrong tool | Yashvanth (×2), Kaven, Yash (SYDE), Aksha, Syra (Aksha's doc), Pereenn | "One number for all of campus is useless to me" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "It doesn't tell him anything about what E7 silent study feels like" (Yash/Ishan). "A campus-specific tool is only worth her time if it provides hyper-local details that general apps miss" (Syra/Aksha). Pereenn said a campus tool would help "be more aware of where to spend her time" in ways a regular app doesn't | 7 of 9 human participants, unprompted | This is the strongest, most consistent finding across all seven documents. Every student independently identified the same gap: the weather app provides ambient outdoor temperature while students need location-specific microclimate data. The tool's entire value proposition rests here. |
| T2. Microclimate variation is severe and location-specific | Yashika (both appearances), Yashvanth, Kaven, Aksha, CE student | "E7 is typically pretty cold because the AC is blasting... the Biology building is actually really hot and muggy" (Yashika/Pahul). "The E5 bridge over Ring Road is a steamy tunnel, it feels nothing like standing right outside DC" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). PAC "varies a lot by time of day, mornings noticeably better" (Yash/Ishan) | 6 of 7 human participants | The most practically important finding for hardware placement. Single-point campus readings are useless. Sensor nodes must go where the microclimate diverges most from ambient conditions: Ring Road crossings, glass bridges, indoor study spaces, PAC, bus stops, outdoor fields. |
| T3. Decisions happen before leaving home, not in the moment | Yashika (SYDE), CE student, Aksha, Anandi, Kaven, Pereenn (Aksha's doc) | "I would check before leaving my house... Things like busyness would help me decide what places to avoid" (Yashika/Yashika). "Let's say I'm getting ready for the day... I would check the conditions" (CE student/Yashika). "I always check the weather before I leave" (Aksha/Syra). Pereenn said being reminded of temperature conditions — like a calendar notification — "would help her plan accordingly" (Pereenn/Aksha) | 6 of 9 human participants | This shapes the product's primary use case. The tool is a pre-departure decision aid, not a mid-campus reference. This means data must be accessible from home (via phone/web), readable in seconds, and presented as recommendations, not raw numbers. Pereenn's preference for passive notifications rather than active app-checking adds a delivery dimension: the tool should come to students, not wait to be opened. |
| T4. Temperature is a proxy; comfort is multi-dimensional | Yash (Ishan's doc), Kaven, Yashika (Pahul's doc), Rose (Jenifer), Rose at Ken's Garden (Aksha), Daylily (Ishan) | "Natural light is actually a bigger factor for him when deciding where to study" (Yash/Ishan). "Wind conditions would be useful for outdoor training" (Kaven/Ishan). "The mild, wet \~20°C day Yashvanth called 'miserable' is the condition this rose most fears" (Jenifer's rose synthesis). Aksha's rose: "a sensor here would need to track more than just ambient air temperature — surface or reflected heat from the adjacent concrete and glass would matter" (Aksha's rose interview) | 4 of 9 human participants; confirmed by 3 more-than-human subjects | Important constraint on feature scope. Multiple participants want data beyond temperature \+ humidity, and the Ken's Garden rose independently corroborates this from a non-human angle — ambient air temperature misses reflected heat off concrete entirely. The design response is to use temp \+ humidity as proxies for comfort rather than displaying raw values, while being honest about what the hardware can't capture. |
| T5. Interface complexity kills adoption | Yashvanth, Anandi, Yash, CE student, Syra (Aksha's doc), Pereenn (Aksha's doc) | "I will not use anything with a login or a download... it has to be as fast as checking the time. Otherwise it's dead to me" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "At a high level, I just want to know... I don't need to know every nitty-gritty detail" (Anandi/Pahul). "Low-friction access is non-negotiable for her. She won't seek out a new platform; it has to fit her existing habits" (Syra/Aksha). Pereenn wants information delivered passively like a calendar reminder, not something she has to go looking for (Pereenn/Aksha) | 6 of 9 human participants | This is the most common product death mode for student projects: technically sophisticated data display that nobody opens. Now confirmed by six independent participants. The interface needs to be faster to check than the native weather app. Zero login, one tap to the relevant information, with a passive notification path for users like Pereenn. |
| T6. Busyness/occupancy is as important as climate | Aksha, Yashika (SYDE), Yashika/CE overlap, Yash | "It would be really helpful if applied to outdoor spaces or study spaces on campus" re: PAC's busyness tracker (Aksha/Syra). "If I make the trip to DC or to E5 and then just realize there's no spots" (Yashika/Yashika). "Overlaying \[temperature\] on top of something that shows the number of people" would be "the perfect system" (CE student/Yashika) | 3 of 7 human participants | This latent need was not stated by most participants; it emerged when they talked about busyness-related frustrations. Occupancy data isn't possible with current hardware constraints. The design response: present temperature \+ humidity as a partial proxy for crowding (a hot, humid study room is often a crowded one). |
| T7. Weather mismatches expectations and causes emotional impact | Aksha, Yashika (SYDE), CE student, Anandi | "It was late May and it 'shouldn't' feel like that. It was the gap between what she expected and what she got that bothered her" (Aksha/Syra). "There's crazy snowstorms... I don't have it in me to step out of the house" — affecting productivity (Yashika/Yashika). "It had been raining and cloudy for several days in a row. Wow, this is why Waterloo is kind of depressing" (CE student/Yashika). "I'm gonna be less motivated" due to rain (Anandi/Pahul) | 4 of 7 human participants | The emotional dimension of campus climate is underappreciated. Weather doesn't just affect route choice; it affects whether students show up at all, and therefore productivity and wellbeing. This suggests the tool's framing should acknowledge "is it worth going today" not just "which route should I take." |
| T8. Desire for the campus to feel designed for its users | Yashvanth, Yash, Kaven, Yashika (Pahul), Pereenn (Aksha's doc) | "I feel like it was 'looking out for me'" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "Facilities should be taking action regarding the uncomfortable spaces rather than students quietly avoiding them" (Yash/Ishan). "The true underlying desire is for a campus that seems designed with all those who live within it in mind" (Ishan's synthesis). Pereenn said a tool that helps her "be more aware of where to spend time at school" and plan around conditions would make campus feel genuinely responsive to students (Pereenn/Aksha) | 5 of 9 human participants (explicit or inferred) | The deepest finding and the one with the highest design leverage. Students don't just want data — they want a campus that acknowledges their experience. A monitoring tool that surfaces uncomfortable conditions and notifies the university's facilities team positions the product as advocacy infrastructure, not just a personal utility. |
| T9. Data is only valuable if it leads to action | Yash (Ishan), Kaven, Yashika (Pahul), Laurel Creek (Yashika) | "Facilities should act on consistently uncomfortable spaces instead of students quietly avoiding them" (Yash/Ishan). "If the data revealed unsafe training conditions, they should add shaded areas, water stations, or reschedule" (Kaven/Ishan). "Data gathering on climate will be useless if it is not used for making any decisions" (Ishan's Theme 4). Laurel Creek: "knowing the soil was dry and doing nothing about it is pointless" | 4 of 7 human participants | This shapes the product vision beyond personal utility. If the tool only helps individual students pick a route, it's nice but limited. If it aggregates data and makes it legible to decision-makers (UW Facilities, Athletics, course schedulers), it becomes infrastructure with lasting impact. Design for both audiences. |
| T10. Students connect local climate to broader climate change | Yashika (SYDE), Yashvanth, Yashika in Yashika's doc | "I wonder if these are actually the impacts of things like climate change, manifesting, improving, or getting worse" (Yashika/Yashika). "The summers are getting genuinely worse. So I guess a tool that makes people actually notice what's happening to their own campus... that's kind of how you start caring?" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). | 2 of 7 human participants (explicit), others implied | Lower frequency but higher resonance. Connecting daily campus observations to longer climate trends is an opportunity for the tool to serve an educational/awareness function. Not a core feature, but a meaningful layer for the "data over time" display. |

---

## ***Important Contradictions***

***Contradiction 1: Detail vs. simplicity** Anandi wants the highest-level signal possible ("tell me what I need to know, nothing more"). Yashika and the CE student both asked for trends, categorized breakdowns, month-on-month comparisons, and overlays. This is a real tension, not a data error. Resolution: the tool needs layered information architecture. A glanceable top-level view (comfort index, color-coded) for Anandi, with drill-down into hourly trends and location comparisons for users like Yashika. Both can be satisfied without conflict.*

***Contradiction 2: Personal utility vs. institutional change** Yashvanth and most students want a quick personal tool — fast, frictionless, decision-ready. Alex (Jenifer's skeptic) explicitly called deeper meaning "overthinking it." But Yash, Kaven, and Ishan's synthesis emphasize that individual benefit is insufficient; the data needs to reach people who can actually fix the uncomfortable spaces. Resolution: design for the individual first (the primary user) and build institutional reporting as a secondary output, not the headline feature.*

***Contradiction 3: High expectations from non-engineers, low friction tolerance from engineers** The non-SYDE students (Sona, Anandi, Alex) have low patience for technical complexity and just want things to work seamlessly. SYDE/engineering students (Yashika, Yashvanth, the CE student) are more willing to engage with data and appreciate trend displays and technical detail. Resolution: the default experience is designed for the non-SYDE student. Engineering-depth views are opt-in.*

***Contradiction 4: Active vs. passive delivery** Syra (Aksha's doc) wants to actively check an app when she needs information — she won't seek out a new platform, but she's happy to open one that fits her existing habits. Pereenn (Aksha's doc) wants the opposite: passive notifications that come to her, compared to how a calendar app sends reminders. Both are right for their own usage patterns, and a tool designed only for one model excludes the other entirely. Resolution: the web dashboard satisfies Syra's check-when-needed habit; a lightweight morning notification (email or webhook) serves Pereenn's passive preference — and keeps it optional so it doesn't irritate users like Syra who don't want to be pushed.*

---

## ***Surprising Observations***

***Pedestrian congestion came up completely unprompted.** Aksha, when asked about environmental information, immediately went to pedestrian traffic density on campus paths — something the design brief doesn't mention at all. She'd already used a busyness tracker for PAC and wanted the same for study spaces and outdoor paths. This was the single most unexpected finding across all six documents.*

***Wind matters more than temperature to commuters.** Yashvanth identified wind specifically — not cold, not wet — as the variable that makes his commute genuinely bad. "Cold I can dress for. Wet I can deal with. But that wind across the open part by Ring Road in January just gets through everything." AHT20 doesn't measure wind. This is worth acknowledging honestly in the design: the hardware constraint means the tool will under-serve commuters on specifically their worst days.*

***One participant (Alex) actively resisted the "meaningful" framing.** Jenifer noted honestly that Alex pushed back on the idea that a weather tool could raise climate awareness or be "meaningful," calling it overthinking. This is the most intellectually honest moment in the six documents. It confirms that any climate-change-awareness layer must be opt-in, not the headline.*

***Multiple interviews converged on the PAC as a case study in variable indoor climate.** PAC's gym temperature varying by time of day was mentioned independently by Yash (Ishan's doc) and Yashika (Pahul's doc). It's the most concrete, specific, and actionable location finding in the dataset.*

***Two team members independently chose the same rose bush at Ken's Garden as their more-than-human subject.** Jenifer and Aksha both interviewed the red rose on Phillip Street without knowing the other would — and produced complementary findings. Jenifer's rose interview focuses on disease risk (humidity, leaf wetness, fungal conditions) and the desire to flourish and be beheld. Aksha's focuses on the physical microclimate of the bed itself — reflected heat off concrete and glass, soil compaction from foot traffic, water retention through the unusual shell mulch. Together they produce the most complete non-human account in the dataset: one team member captured what the rose fears (humid disease weather), the other captured what its immediate physical environment is actually like (a concrete heat sink). Neither finding alone is as strong as both together.*

***The "no picking" sign surfaced independently as an analytic frame in Aksha's document.** The observation that the sign protects the bed's appearance rather than the plant's actual physiological needs — that a bed where nobody picks flowers could still be a bed with compacted soil and heat stress — is a concrete example of what the more-than-human framing is actually for: it catches the gap between human-legible rules and non-human lived reality.*

---

# ***PART 2 — Ten Design Insights***

***Insight 1: The job of this tool is to protect scarce student focus, not provide weather information.** Students do not want to know the temperature — they want to know whether a specific trip to campus is worth making. The tool needs to frame its outputs as go/no-go recommendations, not data readouts.*

*Supporting participants: Yashika (SYDE, Yashika's doc), CE student (Yashika's doc), Aksha (Syra's doc), Pereenn (Aksha's doc) Supporting quotes: "I would check before leaving my house" (Yashika). "The activation energy needed to leave the house is really high" (CE student). "There have been days where she looked at the weather, decided it wasn't worth it, and stayed home" (Aksha/Syra). Pereenn: being reminded of temperature conditions would help her "plan accordingly" — the framing is always forward-looking, before the trip, not during it (Pereenn/Aksha). Why it matters: The primary design target is not a student already on campus — it's a student at home making a decision. Everything about the product's UX, from notification timing to information hierarchy, should serve this moment. Confidence: High (6/9 human participants)*

***Insight 2: A single sensor anywhere on campus produces a reading that is meaningful for students nowhere on campus.** Students don't experience "campus temperature" — they experience the Ring Road crossing, E7 silent study, the PAC locker room, the bus stop on Columbia. Data without location specificity is decorative, not useful.*

*Supporting participants: Yashvanth (×2 docs), Yash (Ishan), Yashika (Pahul), Kaven (Ishan) Supporting quotes: "One number for all of campus is useless to me" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "It has to be connected to specific places" (Ishan's Theme 2 synthesis). "E7 is typically pretty cold... the Biology building is actually really hot and muggy" (Yashika/Pahul). Why it matters: Justifies the multi-node network architecture and directly dictates sensor placement. The design value scales with the number and quality of node locations. Confidence: High (6/7 human participants)*

***Insight 3: Students are solving a comfort problem they expect to be invisible.** Nobody wants to consciously "use a climate tool." They want the climate to feel manageable, the way a well-designed building just feels comfortable without anyone thinking about the HVAC. The tool should work more like an alert system than an app.*

*Supporting participants: Yashvanth (Jenifer), Yash (Ishan), Anandi (Pahul) Supporting quotes: "If it felt like the campus was 'looking out for me'" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "A useful tool would give you the relevant information when you need it, instead of making you look for it" (Ishan's Theme 1). "I just want to know, okay, I'm going out today, what can I expect" (Anandi/Pahul). Why it matters: Pushes the design away from a dashboard-first model and toward a morning push-notification or embedded-in-existing-tools model. If students have to remember to open the tool, adoption will collapse. Confidence: High (4/7 participants explicit; pattern supported by 2 more)*

***Insight 4: The gap between outdoor weather and indoor conditions is the tool's core value, and it remains completely unserved by existing tools.** Every student already checks the weather app. Not one student said the weather app was adequate. The specific gap they all independently named was indoor conditions — the app says 28°C outside, but E7 is 16°C inside and PAC is 32°C.*

*Supporting participants: Yashika (both appearances), Yash (Ishan), CE student (Yashika), Aksha (Syra), Pereenn (Aksha's doc) Supporting quotes: "It would be good to know to dress accordingly" because "buildings will be a lot colder even though the weather outside is nicer" (Yashika/Yashika). "The app doesn't give me data like internally in rooms" (CE student/Yashika). "It would be useful to know which buildings have AC, so she's not surprised when she gets somewhere and it's hot inside" (Aksha/Syra). Pereenn's hot study room incident — she left for a café because the indoor conditions were unbearable, with no way to have known in advance (Pereenn/Aksha). Why it matters: This is the sharpest product differentiation. Competing against the built-in weather app on outdoor conditions would lose. The tool wins by doing the one thing the weather app categorically cannot do: tell you what it feels like inside E7 right now. Pereenn's story is the cleanest example of the exact harm this tool prevents. Confidence: High (6/9 human participants)*

***Insight 5: A number on a screen is not a decision. A recommendation is.** No participant said they wanted to see a temperature reading. They described wanting to know: "should I go?", "which study spot?", "do I need a jacket?", "will the field be usable?" These are recommendations derived from data, not raw data itself.*

*Supporting participants: Anandi (Pahul), Yashvanth (Jenifer), Aksha (Syra), Kaven (Ishan) Supporting quotes: "What I'd actually use is more like a yes/no: 'is it worth walking right now, or do I just wait it out'" (Alex/Jenifer). "A traffic light-style indicator... would be more useful than nothing" (Aksha/Syra). "Translate numbers into plain comfort language" (Yash/Ishan). "Tell me field and commute conditions specifically without digging for it" (Kaven/Ishan). Why it matters: The visualization layer is not decorative — it is where raw data becomes product value. Comfort indices, color coding, and natural-language summaries ("E7 is cool and comfortable right now") convert sensor readings into decisions. This is where design effort should concentrate. Confidence: High (5/7 human participants)*

***Insight 6: Students who move the most around campus have the most to gain from this tool — and are the most underserved.** SYDE students mostly live in E7. Their climate problem is indoor temperature variation between buildings. But students with more distributed days — Science & Business (Alex), Mech Eng commuters (Kaven), Computer Engineering (CE student) — are exposed to outdoor conditions between buildings repeatedly throughout the day. They have the highest pain and no tools designed for them.*

*Supporting participants: Alex (Jenifer), Kaven (Ishan), CE student (Yashika) Supporting quotes: "My classes are spread across totally different buildings, so I'm doing five to fifteen minute walks between them all day. That's the big difference from my engineering friends, they basically live in one room" (Alex/Jenifer). "Commuting on hot days is rough too, arriving on campus already uncomfortable before the day even starts" (Kaven/Ishan). Why it matters: Sensor placement decisions should weight routes between buildings, not just building interiors. The tool is underdesigned if it only serves students who stay in one building. Confidence: Medium (3/7 participants, but strongly supported and distinct from engineering-student data)*

***Insight 7: Students repeatedly make avoidable wasted trips because they can't predict conditions at their destination.** The specific failure pattern: student travels to DC, arrives to find no seats; student walks to SLC, no study rooms available; student goes to PAC in peak heat. Each instance wastes transit time and, more importantly, depletes decision-making energy. The tool prevents wasted trips.*

*Supporting participants: Yashika (SYDE, both appearances), Anandi (Pahul) Supporting quotes: "Sometimes I'll make the trip to DC or to E5 and then just realize there's no spots" (Yashika/Yashika). "I know I don't want to come all the way to DC on a rainy day to find out I can't even find a place to sit" (Anandi/Pahul). "SLC is so far into campus, if I make the trip there and then they don't have a study room, it's definitely really disappointing" (Yashika/Yashika). Why it matters: Avoiding wasted trips is a concrete, measurable user benefit. It makes the tool's value proposition easy to communicate and easy to evaluate. This should be a headline feature in any prototype demonstration. Confidence: Medium-High (3/7 participants explicit; pattern implied in 2 more)*

***Insight 8: Temporal patterns matter as much as current conditions — students need to know if conditions are improving or deteriorating.** Students asked independently for trend views and forecasts: "where is the temperature going to go?" (CE student). "This path is consistently colder in February" (Yashvanth). The time-series data the hardware captures is not just a log — it is a forecast engine if displayed well.*

*Supporting participants: CE student (Yashika), Yashvanth (Jenifer), Yashika (Yashika's doc) Supporting quotes: "I don't like that \[the weather app\] doesn't give you charts and graphs of where the temperature is going to go" (CE student/Yashika). "Something that shows you the pattern, like 'this path is consistently colder in February,' that would actually help people who aren't here all year" (Yashvanth/Jenifer). "Month-on-month" spending breakdowns as an analogy for climate data (Yashika/Yashika). Why it matters: The SD card data logging in Labs 4 and 5 generates exactly the longitudinal dataset these participants are asking for. Displaying it as a trend is a direct translation of hardware capability into user value. This is a core feature, not a nice-to-have. Confidence: Medium (3/7 explicit; logically important)*

***Insight 9: The more-than-human data establishes that the same sensor readings mean completely different things to different stakeholders.** A mild, wet 20°C June day is "miserable but manageable" to Yashvanth and "onset of fungal disease weather" to the rose. This is not a quirk — it is a design principle. A single reading decoded differently for different audiences is a fundamentally richer product than a single reading decoded once.*

*Supporting participants: Jenifer's synthesis (rose vs. Yashvanth), Syra's synthesis (Canada Goose), Ishan's synthesis (Daylily) Key insight: The same temperature and humidity value triggers different actions for: a student choosing a jacket, an athlete deciding about outdoor training, a grounds crew checking fungal disease risk, and a campus administrator reviewing energy use. The hardware captures one dataset; the value multiplies with the number of audiences who can interpret it through their own lens. Why it matters: Justifies layered display modes (student view, institutional view, ecological view) without requiring additional hardware. Also provides the strongest answer to the course's "more-than-human" design imperative. Confidence: Medium (inferred from more-than-human synthesis; not directly testable with human participants)*

***Insight 10: The tool's long-term value depends entirely on whether anyone takes ownership of it after the course ends.** Alex (Jenifer's doc) asked the most practically important question in all six interviews: "who's going to keep it running?" A distributed sensor network with no maintainer becomes "just e-waste in two years." Designing for maintainability — simple wiring, easy battery replacement, low-cost components, documented setup — is as important as designing the user experience.*

*Supporting participants: Alex (Jenifer) Supporting quote: "I'd want to know... is anyone going to actually maintain this? Because a network with no owner is just e-waste in two years." (Alex/Jenifer, paraphrased from interview synthesis) Why it matters: Most student project tools never get used after grading. Designing explicitly for handoff — to UW Sustainability, UW Facilities, or a future SYDE cohort — extends the tool's impact and impresses evaluators who think about systems, not just prototypes. Confidence: Low-Medium (one participant, but the concern is structurally correct and widely applicable)*

***Insight 11: Ecological stewardship is a designed-in feature, not a natural student instinct — but the physical evidence for it is immediate, not abstract.** Across Aksha's two human interviews, neither Syra nor Pereenn raised non-human campus life unprompted. Both connected with it quickly once it was framed concretely around geese and wildlife habitats. Meanwhile Aksha's rose — a few metres from where students walk daily — has survival needs (protection from reflected heat, uncompacted soil, water reaching its roots) that the tool would already be positioned to track, without any additional hardware.*

*Supporting participants: Syra (Aksha's doc), Pereenn (Aksha's doc), Ken's Garden rose (Aksha and Jenifer) Supporting quotes: "She hadn't thought about the more-than-human aspect before I asked, but she connected with it quickly" (Syra/Aksha). "She said this was something she would not normally think about on her own, and that it would be nice to have a platform that included this kind of information" (Pereenn/Aksha). Aksha's rose: "the stuff a tool would need to track for ecological stewardship — soil compaction, reflected heat off pavement, water reaching root zones — isn't some abstract hypothetical. It's the actual baseline reality for a real plant a few meters from where students walk every single day" (Aksha's cross-cutting observation). Why it matters: This reframes the more-than-human design requirement from a philosophical exercise into a concrete product feature. The ecological layer doesn't require additional hardware or separate infrastructure — it requires a different framing of the same data. Calling the temperature+humidity reading at an outdoor node "current heat stress conditions for nearby plantings" costs nothing extra and extends the tool's relevance to grounds staff and sustainability advocates. Confidence: Medium (consistent across Aksha and Jenifer's more-than-human interviews; human uptake is prompted not spontaneous)*

# ***PART 3 — Insights to Features***

| *Feature* | *Motivating Insight(s)* | *Hardware Required* | *Software Required* | *Difficulty* | *User Impact* | *Feasibility (by July 27\)* | *Priority* |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| ***Multi-node sensor network** (3–5 ESP32+AHT20 nodes at distinct locations)* | *I1, I2, I4* | *ESP32 × 3–5, AHT20 × 3–5, microSD × 3–5, buck converters × 3–5, 9V batteries × 3–5* | *Data collection firmware per node (Lab 4/5 code adapted)* | *Medium* | *Critical — no tool without this* | *High* | *Essential* |
| ***Location-specific comfort index** (translate raw temp/humidity to plain-English comfort tier: Cool, Comfortable, Warm, Hot, Humid)* | *I5, I2* | *None beyond above* | *Comfort calculation algorithm (HI or simplified thermal comfort)* | *Low-Medium* | *High — turns data into decisions* | *High* | *Essential* |
| ***Web dashboard with campus map** (color-coded nodes overlaid on campus map, readable at a glance)* | *I3, I5, I6* | *Wifi-capable ESP32, or central data hub* | *Web frontend (HTML/CSS/JS), hosted locally or via ESP32 webserver* | *Medium* | *Critical for adoption* | *Medium-High* | *Essential* |
| ***Pre-departure check view** (mobile-optimized single-page view designed for morning "should I go?" decision)* | *I1, I3* | *None beyond above* | *Responsive mobile layout, comfort summary per key location* | *Low* | *High — serves primary use case* | *High* | *Essential* |
| ***24-hour trend display per node** (simple line chart of temperature and humidity over the past 24 hours)* | *I8* | *microSD (already required)* | *Chart.js or D3 front-end; parse SD data server-side* | *Medium* | *Medium-High — shows patterns not just snapshots* | *Medium* | *Essential* |
| ***Natural-language daily summary** (auto-generated sentence: "E7 is 17°C with low humidity — cool, bring a layer")* | *I5, I3* | *None beyond above* | *String template logic fed from comfort tiers* | *Low* | *High — most accessible display format* | *High* | *Nice-to-Have* |
| ***Comfort comparison across nodes** (side-by-side view: "Right now, E7 is cooler than DC")* | *I2, I7* | *None beyond above* | *Comparative display logic* | *Low* | *Medium* | *High* | *Nice-to-Have* |
| ***Historical baseline comparison** (this week vs. last week, or this reading vs. the seasonal average for this location)* | *I8, I10* | *microSD (already required — data accumulates)* | *Date-range query and averaging logic* | *Medium* | *Medium — adds depth for power users* | *Medium* | *Nice-to-Have* |
| ***Institutional data export** (CSV export or dashboard page showing weekly trends for Facilities or Athletics)* | *I9, I10* | *None beyond above* | *Export endpoint or secondary display page* | *Low-Medium* | *Low for students, potentially high for institutional impact* | *High* | *Nice-to-Have* |
| ***Enclosure for portable deployment** (3D-printed or laser-cut housing to weatherproof one outdoor node)* | *All (reliability)* | *Enclosure materials* | *None* | *Medium-High* | *Medium — required for outdoor deployment* | *Medium* | *Nice-to-Have* |
| ***Push notification / email alert** (alert when a key location hits extreme conditions)* | *I3, I1* | *None beyond above* | *Notification backend (email API or webhook)* | *High* | *Medium* | *Low-Medium* | *Stretch Goal* |
| ***Ecological display layer** (reframe outdoor node readings as heat stress / comfort conditions for nearby plantings; relevant to grounds crew and UW Sustainability)* | *I11, I9* | *None beyond above* | *Secondary interpretation layer; label toggle in the dashboard* | *Low (once built)* | *Low for students, meaningful for institutional demo and more-than-human design credit* | *High* | *Nice-to-Have* |

***Features deliberately excluded:***

* ***Occupancy / busyness data:** Multiple participants wanted this, but it requires camera or people-counting sensors not in the hardware kit and raises privacy concerns. Acknowledge the gap in the design rationale; don't pretend temperature proxies occupancy perfectly.*  
* ***Air quality / CO₂ monitoring:** Mentioned tangentially in Yashika's CE interview and in some more-than-human contexts. Different sensor hardware entirely. Out of scope.*  
* ***Wind data:** Yashvanth's most requested variable. AHT20 doesn't measure wind speed. Name this gap honestly.*  
* ***Login/user accounts:** Yashvanth drew a hard line. Any authentication layer will kill adoption. Zero login.*

# ***PART 4 — Recommended Final Prototype***

## ***"Campus Compass" — A Distributed Microclimate Intelligence Network for UW Students***

### ***Product Overview***

*Campus Compass is a network of 4–5 battery-powered ESP32 nodes deployed at the locations on campus where students' lived experience of climate diverges most from ambient weather readings. Each node measures temperature and humidity every 60 seconds, logs data to a microSD card, and transmits readings to a central ESP32 hub via WiFi. The hub serves a lightweight web dashboard accessible from any phone or laptop on UWaterloo WiFi — no login, no app download, opens in a browser in under two seconds.*

*The dashboard's primary view is a campus map with color-coded indicators showing current comfort levels at each node location (blue \= cool/comfortable, amber \= warm, red \= hot/humid). Below the map, each location card shows a one-sentence natural-language summary ("E7 silent study is 16°C — bring a layer") and a 24-hour sparkline. A secondary view shows trend data over the past week for users who want to understand patterns, not just snapshots.*

### ***Recommended Node Locations (based on interview evidence)***

*These are the locations named most frequently and specifically across the six interviews, and the ones where students described the greatest gap between what the weather app says and what they actually experience:*

1. ***E7 silent study (indoor)** — Most-mentioned specific location; students repeatedly described it as reliably cold regardless of outdoor conditions*  
2. ***Ring Road crossing / bus stop on Columbia** — Yashvanth named this twice as the worst wind exposure on his commute; Kaven noted the bus route exposure*  
3. ***PAC fitness area (indoor)** — Named independently by Yash (Ishan), Yashika (Pahul), and Aksha (Syra) as variably hot/humid; "mornings noticeably better"*  
4. ***DC study floor (indoor)** — Named by Yashika (SYDE), Yash (Ishan), and Anandi as cold and "depressing" in winter; wasted trips when full*  
5. ***SLC / QNC outdoor courtyard (outdoor)** — The contrast location: where students sit when conditions allow; baseline for "it's actually nice outside today"*

---

### ***Target Users***

***Primary:** UW students in their first session on campus or returning from co-op who don't have an intuitive sense of which spaces are comfortable when. Yashvanth specifically named this: "Every time I come back from co-op I've completely forgotten which routes are awful in which season."*

***Secondary:** Students making high-stakes study spot decisions on exam weeks when a wasted trip has real academic cost.*

***Tertiary (institutional):** UW Facilities staff who can use aggregated trend data to identify consistently uncomfortable spaces worth fixing. UW Athletics for outdoor training scheduling.*

---

### ***Key User Journey***

***6:30 AM — Sofia is a 2A student with a 9 AM class and three hours of studying planned afterward.** She opens her phone and navigates to `campus-compass.local` (or a shared link). In under 5 seconds she sees: E7 is currently 15°C (cool — bring a layer); DC is 19°C (comfortable); the Ring Road bus stop area is flagged amber (warm and humid today, unusual for this time). She decides to head to DC instead of E7, puts on a lighter jacket, and leaves home with no surprises.*

***9:30 AM — Between lectures, Sofia's study group wants to meet. One person suggests SLC.** She pulls up Campus Compass and sees SLC outdoor area is 27°C / 70% RH (flagged warm). Someone checks DC — still comfortable from this morning. They go to DC.*

***That evening — Sofia's friend on the Athletics team, Kaven, has practice at 6 PM.** He checks Campus Compass for the SLC outdoor area (as a proxy for field conditions). It reads 29°C / 65% RH. The natural-language summary says: "Warm and humid — stay hydrated, best before 4 PM or after 7 PM." He brings extra water and starts practice at 5:30.*

---

### ***Why This Solves a Real Problem***

*The weather app gives Sofia one number for all of Waterloo. Campus Compass gives her five readings at the five places she actually goes. The difference isn't incremental — it's the difference between "it's 21°C outside" (useless for Sofia's decisions) and "E7 is freezing, DC is fine, the Ring Road is brutal today" (actionable in 3 seconds).*

*The product doesn't compete with Weather.com. It does the one thing Weather.com categorically cannot do: tell you what it feels like inside a specific building right now.*

### ***Why This Is More Compelling Than a Standard Weather Station***

*A standard weather station puts one sensor on a roof and displays a number. Campus Compass puts sensors where students actually are, displays information through a lens of comfort rather than raw data, and frames every reading as an answer to the question students are actually asking: "should I go there, and what should I bring?"*

*The distributed architecture, the comfort index translation layer, the natural-language summaries, and the 24-hour trend display all come directly from what participants asked for. No feature in this prototype was invented — every one of them was requested by at least one participant in language this specific.*

### ***What Would Impress Instructors at Critique***

1. ***Justified node placement.** Being able to say "this sensor is here because three independent participants named this exact location" is a stronger design argument than any other justification.*

2. ***The comfort index, not raw numbers.** Displaying a color-coded comfort tier and a natural-language sentence demonstrates that you understood the difference between data and a product.*

3. ***The pre-departure use case.** Framing the demo around "here's what a student does at 7 AM at home before leaving" is a stronger narrative than "here's our sensor array."*

4. ***Honest acknowledgment of what the tool can't do.** Naming the gaps — no wind data, no occupancy data, no air quality — and explaining why (hardware constraints \+ interview-grounded scope decisions) demonstrates research maturity. Don't hide the gaps; foreground them as evidence of scope discipline.*

5. ***The institutional angle as a secondary audience.** Briefly showing the CSV export or trend dashboard and saying "this data is also useful to UW Facilities" positions the prototype as infrastructure rather than a school project.*

### ***Ideas That Should Not Be Included (and Why)***

***Gamification or loyalty features.** Several participants wanted simple tools, not engagement mechanics. Adding streaks, badges, or leaderboards would confuse the product's identity without serving any stated need.*

***Real-time alerts / push notifications.** The backend complexity required significantly exceeds the benefit given timeline constraints. Users check the tool voluntarily before leaving home; they don't need push alerts for this use case.*

***Predictive ML / forecasting beyond 24 hours.** Would require training data you don't have in the timeframe, and participants didn't ask for predictions — they asked for current conditions and recent trends. Scope this out.*

***On-device display (OLED screen on each node).** Adds hardware complexity and cost with low user benefit — no participant described wanting to walk up to a physical sensor and read a screen. The phone dashboard serves the actual use case.*

***A custom mobile app.** Requires app store distribution and device installation. Yashvanth's "I will not use anything with a download" rule applies. A responsive web app is the right answer.*

---

## ***Contingency Plan***

***If node count slips below 5:** Deploy 3 nodes at the highest-priority locations (E7, DC, PAC). Accept reduced coverage; the tool still works, and the demo is still compelling. Do not rush hardware assembly at the cost of stability.*

***If WiFi hub architecture is too complex:** Fall back to manual SD card data collection (pull the card, copy data to laptop, push to hosted dashboard daily). Loses real-time functionality but preserves all other features.*

***If critique feedback redirects the design:** Reserve July 7–8 as full-team design response days before beginning feature build. Don't code features until the critique feedback is digested.*

***Hardware reserve:** Assign Jenifer or Syra as hardware backup team — should be able to re-solder, reconfigure, or replace a failing node if needed.*

# ***PART 6 — Plan & Check-In Document Draft***

*Following the D4 requirements document (1 page Check-In \+ 2 pages Planning \+ 1 page Dreaming)*

---

## ***Section 1: Check-In (Where are we at?)***

***What we've done:***

*Seven independent Needs & Desires assessments, each including a SYDE classmate interview, a non-SYDE UW student interview, and a more-than-human interview. Across fourteen human participants and six non-human stakeholders, we identified five major recurring themes:*

* *Students at UW rely on the native weather app as their primary climate tool, but it categorically fails to answer the questions they're actually asking (indoor conditions, route-specific microclimate, pre-departure decisions)*  
* *Microclimate variation between locations on campus is severe and well-documented: E7 silent study, PAC, the Ring Road crossings, and DC were all named independently across multiple interviews as having reliably anomalous conditions*  
* *The primary use moment is before leaving home, not on campus — the tool needs to support a 5-second morning check, not a mid-campus lookup; Pereenn's preference for passive calendar-style notifications adds a second delivery pattern beyond active app-checking*  
* *Students want comfort information translated into decisions, not raw temperature and humidity values*  
* *Ecological stewardship is not a spontaneous student instinct but it connects quickly when framed concretely — and the physical evidence for it is already present in the same locations the tool would monitor. Two team members independently chose the same rose bush at Ken's Garden as their more-than-human subject, producing complementary findings about disease risk and reflected heat that together make the strongest non-human case in the dataset*

***Design focus:***

*We are building a distributed microclimate monitoring network — 5 ESP32+AHT20 nodes at the campus locations where students' lived experience of climate diverges most from ambient weather readings. The system serves a lightweight web dashboard that presents conditions as color-coded comfort indices and natural-language summaries, designed for a 5-second pre-departure check on a phone.*

*This is not a weather station. It is a decision tool for students who want to know whether going to campus is worth the trip today, and which spaces to choose when they get there.*

---

## ***Section 2: Planning (Where are we going this term?)***

### ***Design Objectives (prioritized)***

1. ***Reliable multi-node data collection** — 5 functional nodes, stable at 60-second intervals, minimum 8-hour battery life per node. Without this, nothing else exists.*

2. ***Comfort index display with location specificity** — temperature and humidity translated into a comfort tier (Cool / Comfortable / Warm / Hot / Humid) and a plain-English summary per location. This is the core product value.*

3. ***Mobile-optimized web dashboard** — zero login, opens on UWaterloo WiFi, legible in under 5 seconds. Yashvanth drew a hard line: "I will not use anything with a login or a download. It has to be as fast as checking the time."*

4. ***24-hour trend visualization** — a sparkline per location showing the last 24 hours. The SD card logging in Labs 4 and 5 generates this data automatically; displaying it costs relatively little and adds significant depth.*

5. ***Documentation for handoff** — wiring diagrams, firmware setup guide, sensor placement rationale. Design for a future SYDE cohort or UW Facilities to maintain the network after the course ends.*

***Nice-to-have (if time permits):** Historical baseline comparison, institutional CSV export, outdoor-rated enclosure for Ring Road node.*

***Out of scope (evidence-grounded decisions):** Push notifications (backend complexity, low adoption benefit), occupancy data (different hardware), wind speed (different sensor), login/accounts (kills adoption per participant feedback).*

### ***How We'll Get There***

*See Part 5 project timeline above. Key milestones:*

| *Date* | *Milestone* |
| ----- | ----- |
| *June 27* | *Hardware inventory confirmed, single node proof-of-concept working* |
| *June 30* | *2 nodes working; dashboard serving live data from 1 node* |
| *July 4* | *5 nodes built and tested; dashboard fully integrated* |
| *July 6* | *Design Critique* |
| *July 13* | *All 5 nodes deployed at target locations; trend display complete* |
| *July 20* | *Historical view, mobile polish, user walkthrough test complete* |
| *July 27* | *Final prototype submission* |

---

## ***Section 3: Dreaming (Short and Long Term)***

### ***Short term (1–2 years): Campus Compass as UW Infrastructure***

*In the near term, Campus Compass stops being a student project and becomes something UW actually maintains. The node network grows from 5 locations to 20, covering every major student destination: Laurel Creek Trail, SLC study floors, the E3 courtyard, the bus terminals. The dashboard gets a university-branded interface and appears in the UWaterloo app alongside room bookings and shuttle schedules. Students start each morning with a "Campus Compass forecast" the way they start with the weather forecast — except this one tells them things the weather forecast never could.*

*UW Facilities begins using the longitudinal trend data. For the first time, they can see empirically that E7 silent study consistently runs below 17°C in summer while students are dressed for outdoor heat. Instead of relying on anecdotal complaints, they can point to 14 weeks of 6 AM to midnight sensor data and make an HVAC adjustment that 800 students per day benefit from, and none of them ever know about. That's the version of this tool that actually changes the campus. \[\[None of what is being built will last if it isn’t maintained so in order to avoid e-waste we need solar-charged, easily swappable nodes and we need to close the wind/lighting gaps that Yash flagged. A full season of field data would also help UW Athletics have real evidence for practice conditions in order to establish the necessary infrastructure as opposed to leaving athletes like Kaven to accommodate on their own.\]\]*

### ***Long term (100–500 years): Infrastructure that Remembers***

*In the far future, a campus that monitors itself has already become unremarkable infrastructure — temperature sensors embedded in sidewalks and study tables are no more notable than motion-activated lights. But what the long-term version of Campus Compass accumulates is a longitudinal record of place.*

*In 2126, a historian could pull 100 years of temperature and humidity data from E7 and see: the years when Waterloo summers were still gentle; the decade when heat events started exceeding 35°C regularly; the period when the university retrofitted solar shading on south-facing buildings and the indoor sensor readings at E7 dropped by 3°C permanently. The data would not just show conditions — it would show decisions, and their consequences.*

*The more-than-human subjects in our research — Laurel Creek, Silver Lake, the rose bush at Ken's Garden — are participants in the same climate their human neighbors navigate. In 200 years, if the sensor network has expanded to the creek banks and the campus wetlands, the record would hold both the human experience of a place and the ecological health of the watershed it sits in. These are not separate stories. They are the same story, measured at different heights. \[Over a few decades, the field data would shift from individual athlete’s judgement call to real evidence. A long enough record would be established that UW Athletics could point to actual patterns to ensure ideal conditions for athletes as opposed to relying on anecdotes.\]*

*This is what the more-than-human framing from our course readings — Krenak's warning that we must hear the voices of all beings, Kopenawa's vision of a living forest, Haraway's insistence on making kin rather than just making data — points toward. Not a tool that monitors nature for human convenience, but a system of attention that holds the full community of a place in record, human and otherwise, across geological time.* 

*We begin with five sensors and five locations. We begin with the campus we have. The data starts now.*

*Ideas:*

- *Energy waste indicator: indoor vs outdoor temp differential per node, we can save energy by showing buildings over-heating or cooling relative to outside*  
- *Commit to long term data retention to show evidence over time and not just week-to-week comparisons*  
- *Cross node comparisons over seasons to show how indoor/outdoor gap varies across seasons in the year (shows climate impact on buildings and not just extreme days)*

---

## 1.0 Check In {#1.0-check-in}

We've completed the Needs and Desires phase of our project by conducting interviews with University of Waterloo students, synthesizing the findings across participants, and comparing them with existing environmental monitoring tools. This helped us identify recurring pain points and narrow our project to a clear design direction.

One finding came up in almost every interview: students rely on weather apps when deciding where to study or travel on campus. The problem is that weather apps only report outdoor conditions, while indoor spaces can feel completely different depending on occupancy, HVAC systems, and building design. Several participants described arriving at study spaces that were much hotter, colder, or less comfortable than they expected. We also reviewed existing resources, including weather apps and campus services, and found that none provide room-level environmental information. This confirmed that our interviews had identified a real gap rather than an isolated concern.

We also noticed two other consistent trends. First, participants wanted a solution that was quick and effortless to use, with several saying they would avoid a tool that required logging in or navigating through multiple screens. Second, participants found raw environmental data difficult to interpret and preferred information that directly helped them make decisions. Our more-than-human interviews also highlighted interest in environmental stewardship, suggesting that ecological information could add value as a secondary feature without distracting from the primary goal of improving student comfort.

These findings shaped the priorities for our design. Rather than building another weather application, we are designing a system that provides location-specific comfort information, presents it in a simple and intuitive way, and remains accessible with as little friction as possible. 

In the build phase, we plan to prototype three nodes which will be located in areas that users cited most frequently in our interviews (E7 silent study, DC, and the SLC courtyard). Building three nodes allows us to focus on the most frequently mentioned indoor anomaly sites, as well as one outdoor site for contrast. Each node will consist of one ESP32 and one AHT20 Sensor that will be enclosed in the custom enclosure seen in the Appendix. It will continuously read temperature/humidity every 60 seconds, log that data to a microSD card, and send that data back to a central ESP32 hub via UWaterloo WiFi. The hub will have a lightweight web dashboard (without requiring any login or app to download), with the primary view being a campus map with colour-coded comfort indicators (blue \= cool & comfortable; amber \= warm; red \= hot & humid). Below each campus location card, there will be a one-line overview of the location such as “E7 Silent Study is 16°c – Bring a Layer”, along with a 24-hour sparkline, and the second view will show the previous week-long trend.

Layered onto the core functionality, we also plan to include a few more features:

- A "what to wear" recommendation based off of today's reading  
- A light predictive ability that can predict through humidity a possible rain event  
- A CO2 based occupancy indicator to prevent students from making wasted trips to an occupied room. 

With our research synthesized and our system architecture defined, we're moving into the build phase; the section below lays out our prioritized outcomes, timeline, and task assignments for the rest of the term.

## 2.0 Planning {#2.0-planning}

This term, our goal is to take Campus Compass from a validated concept, grounded in our Needs & Desires research, to a working hardware and software prototype ready for final submission. Our research pointed us toward a clear direction: students don't want another weather app, they want to know what a specific place on campus feels like right now, before they decide whether it's worth going there. Our design focus this term is on building toward that gap, a distributed sensor network that captures hyper-local temperature and humidity conditions across campus, paired with a lightweight dashboard that translates those readings into a comfort-based recommendation students can act on in a few seconds. Over the course of the term we need to design and build out the full system end to end, from breadboarding and firmware through PCB fabrication, enclosure design, and the web dashboard, while prioritizing the highest-risk components, like electronics reliability and PCB manufacturing lead time, early enough that problems there don't jeopardize the rest of the build.

What are you planning to get done this term? Provide a prioritized list of project outcomes.

1. **Reliable multi-node data collection**: a network of ESP32 \+ AHT20 nodes logging temperature and humidity reliably at regular intervals with reasonable battery life.  
2. **Comfort index translation layer**:  raw temperature and humidity readings converted into a plain-English comfort tier (e.g. Cool / Comfortable / Warm / Hot / Humid) rather than displayed as raw numbers. This is the core product insight from our research: students consistently told us they want a recommendation, not a raw reading.  
3. **Web dashboard**: zero login, fast load time, accessible on UWaterloo WiFi. A hard requirement based directly on participant feedback that any friction would kill adoption.  
4. **PCB design and fabrication**: moving off breadboards onto a soldered PCB per node, prioritized early since manufacturing lead time is our biggest scheduling risk.  
5. **Custom enclosure (CAD)**: a 3D-printed or laser-cut housing that protects each node outdoors while keeping the sensor ventilated.  
6. **24-hour trend visualization**: a sparkline per node location, since our SD card logging generates this data automatically and multiple participants specifically asked for pattern data, not just a snapshot.  
7. **Verification & validation testing**: sensor accuracy against a reference thermometer/hygrometer, 24-hour logging reliability, WiFi reconnection behaviour, and battery endurance.  
8. **Physical deployment:** installing units at our confirmed target locations across campus.

We plan on ensuring that we’re able to meet our project purpose and design focus by following our project plan, tracked as a board on Google Sheets, and meeting once a week to touch-base on task progression. To devise our project plan, we started by breaking down our project into key steps, from which we classified specific workstreams. These workstreams help us track key project milestones, and are are grouped as follows: CAD design (box), PCB design (all electronic components), web app design, breadboarding, Arduino code writing, testing on breadboard, assembly (soldering components to PCBs \+ putting into box \+ assembling), final prototype testing, and the demo video. 

These workstream groupings ensure that multiple project topics are able to be completed in parallel by workstream pairs (e.g. one pair working on CAD while another works on PCB design). We have planned and set deadlines for workstream milestones as per chronological order and dependencies \-- e.g. testing the first breadboard with Arduino code early on, prior to PCB design, to ensure that the code is working and feasible. This plan is as follows (full project found [here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1vhLOYaInnmqE-ZmZ9ZWyuajkHLCShk9RheqDHhVeM-E/edit?gid=663629714#gid=663629714)):  
 ![][image3]  
![][image4]  
![][image5]  
Figure 1: Project Plan Task Board

As tasks are started, the “Status” dropdown will be updated and this also allows transparency across all group members, especially for those who are dependent on other tasks to be done. In addition to weekly meetings, we also have a group chat to discuss any time-sensitive blockers or queries and ensure that they are resolved in a timely manner.

## 3.0 Dreaming {#3.0-dreaming}

**Short term (1-2 years)**  
In two years, Campus Compass is boring, and that is the dream. The student checks their phone at 7 AM and sees that E7 is 16°C, the Ring Road intersection is treacherous, and the SLC courtyard is a worthwhile diversion. Zero log-in, zero download, zero effort. Our research indicated that the quickest way to end this tool was to make it seem like a tool, so the immediate dream is invisibility, the campus passively "looking out for you," in the words of one participant.  
Below the surface of that boredom, however, something else gathers: an ongoing, microlocal archive of how this campus really feels to the bodies navigating it. According to Korsmeyer et al., the future is contained within the present, and part of what dreaming means is learning to see it \[j\]. In 2028, that archive lies in front of UW Facilities, such that the spaces that students have found ways around can be seen by those who have the budget to fix them, while humidity data at ground level alerts caretakers of disease-risk periods. Critically, the network has an owner, because as our most skeptical interviewee put it, a network with no owner "is just e-waste in two years." The two-year dream ends with a handoff, not a demo.  
**Long term (100-500 years)**  
No dataset reaches 2526, hence the dreaming must be done through data fiction, defined by Maciejko and Lecuna as imaginary data whose value is not the predictability but rather the speculation enabled by it \[s\]. Here’s ours. Humid morning of June in 2426\. The node at the garden bed on Phillip Street displays 20°C and wet conditions – conditions that students in 2026 described as “miserable” and which rose beds growing there felt as the conditions for fungal disease weather. Four centuries later, the reading of one sensor is decoded by everyone affected by it,  irrigation and air circulation systems are adjusted so that the black spot is prevented from taking off, walking areas moved to the shades, and the student will never think about any of this consciously.  
Such near-milestones are not made up out of thin air: Waterloo itself has officially declared a state of emergency regarding its climate and through its Shift:Neutral plan, aims to become carbon neutral by 2050, which is partially possible due to the efficient renovations and monitoring of the environment of its current buildings \[z\]. The milestone of 2050 is not an alternate reality separate from the one of our university; rather, it is the exact same date. We dream deliberately into an uncomfortable future because, as Nilsson et al. show, futures with limits reveal needs that comfortable scenarios never do \[y\]: hotter summer temperatures, scarce energy supplies, habitability as a public health issue. And since futures have always been written by the dominant \[f\], the deepest impact comes from including those who were erased, and the most erased stakeholders on our campus have no voice at all \[x\].  
But the honest 500-year dream cannot be a bigger network; our own needs assessment warned that a climate monitor can become one more screen between people and the beings around them. The rose survived millions of years without a sensor; what keeps it alive is someone paying attention. So the furthest milestone is the strangest: the network completes its work by becoming unnecessary (the falling curve in Fig. 1). By 2526 the noticing has become culture and the sensors fade, needed less each decade. Success is not how often the dashboard is opened but how little it needs to be. We build only what helps this place hear its other beings, never what helps it forget them. Three ESP32 nodes logging every sixty seconds is where that habit starts.  
![][image6]  
*Fig. 2\. Back-cast for Campus Compass \[x\]: the 2526 end state is defined first. Reliance on the tool rises then falls to zero as noticing becomes cultural habit and remains. Axis not to scale.*

\[2\] M. Maciejko and A. Lecuna, "Post-Futures: A Speculative Design Method Integrating Data Fiction for Design-Futures Education," in Proc. EKSIG 2025: Data as Experiential Knowledge and Embodied Processes, Budapest, Hungary, May 2025, doi: 10.21606/eksig2025.123.

\[3\] University of Waterloo, "Shift:Neutral: The University of Waterloo's Roadmap to Carbon Neutrality," Waterloo, ON, Canada, 2020\. \[Online\]. ## 1.0 Check In {#1.0-check-in-1}

We completed the Needs and Desires phase of our project by conducting interviews with University of Waterloo students, synthesizing recurring themes across participants, and comparing our findings with existing environmental monitoring tools. When ideating, we initially thought about how to help students identify comfortable places to study by providing location-specific environmental information. However, after analyzing our interview findings and reflecting on Professor Borland’s feedback, we realized the data our system collects has the potential to support a broader climate-monitoring purpose beyond individual comfort.

Across our interviews, students consistently described noticeable environmental differences between locations on campus. They pointed out that weather apps provide a single temperature reading for Waterloo but fail to capture the localized conditions created by tree cover, concrete, building materials, and shading. While students experience these differences every day, there is currently no way to objectively compare how different campus environments respond to weather. Our more-than-human interviews reinforced this observation by highlighting clear environmental differences between vegetated spaces and nearby paved areas, suggesting these contrasting environments provide an opportunity to study campus microclimates rather than simply report weather conditions.

As a result, our design focus has shifted toward translating hyper-local environmental data into actionable insights for Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning students. We plan to investigate how features of the built environment such as vegetation, paving materials, and surrounding buildings influence campus microclimates by identifying localized heat sinks and cooler vegetated areas. By making these patterns visible, we hope to support climate-conscious design decisions for future campus planning, including opportunities for increased tree canopy, improved shading, and other heat mitigation strategies.

Since this represents a shift in our primary stakeholder, we also recognize the need to validate this direction through additional user research. Moving forward, we plan to interview students in Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning (included in our project plan) to better understand what environmental information is most valuable to their design process and ensure our prototype generates insights that are both meaningful and actionable for these users.

Our design objective of converting hyper-local environmental data into useful information for Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning students led us to the decision to build a physical system that monitors heat sinks on campus. The node consists of an ESP32 and AHT20 sensor to measure ambient temperature and humidity, a MLX90614 non-invasive IR thermometer for measuring surface temperatures, and a BH1750 to measure lux for solar exposure. These four devices will be wired to the same I2C bus for overall simplicity of design. By comparing a paved or built surface against a nearby vegetated or shaded control point, the node captures how much hotter exposed surfaces run relative to ambient conditions, and how that gap relates to sun exposure throughout the day. A significant difference in ambient and surface temperature show where an active heat sink is located. Conversely, a lesser difference indicates that existing vegetation/shaded area is already mitigating heat significantly. This gives us a concrete, evidence-based way to determine where campus surfaces are retaining and radiating heat. These readings will be presented through a lightweight, no-login web dashboard, showing the surface-versus-control differential over time so the data is easy for students to interpret at a glance rather than requiring someone to parse raw sensor values.

## 2.0 Planning {#2.0-planning-1}

This term, our goal is to take the pivoted design from a validated need to a working prototype that demonstrates real actionable insight for an Architectural Engineering or Urban Planning audience, with the purpose of designing a greener UW campus in the future. That means building a distributed sensor network capturing surface-level and solar-exposure signals, not just ambient air, paired with a dashboard that translates those signals into decisions a planner or building designer could act on, while prioritizing the highest-risk components early enough to avoid jeopardizing the rest of the build. Over the course of the term we need to design and build out the full system end to end, from breadboarding and firmware to PCB fabrication, enclosure design, and the web dashboard.

Our prioritized list of project outcomes includes:

9. **Validate the pivot with target users:** short interviews with Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning students this weekend, confirming the surface-vs-control framing is actually useful to them before we build further around it.  
10. **Instrumented heat sink node at a paved site:** ESP32 \+ AHT20 (ambient temperature/humidity) \+ MLX90614 (non-contact surface temperature) \+ BH1750 (solar exposure), sharing one I2C bus to keep wiring simple.  
11. **Paired vegetated control point:** a second, lighter-weight measurement point near the node so every reading has something to be compared against, since a single number in isolation isn't something a planner can act on.  
12. **Surface-vs-ambient differential as the core metric:** the dashboard's job is to answer "is this surface retaining and re-radiating heat relative to its surroundings," rather than displaying raw sensor values.  
13. **PCB design and fabrication:** moved off the breadboard early given PCB shipping risks.  
14. **Enclosure design (CAD):** housing that shields electronics while leaving the AHT20/BH1750 exposed to ambient conditions and keeping a clear line of sight for the MLX90614.  
15. **Web dashboard showing differential over time:** zero login, fast load, built for someone evaluating a site's behaviour over days, not a single moment.  
16. **Verification & validation testing:** surface and ambient sensor accuracy against reference instruments, logging reliability, WiFi reconnection, and battery endurance.  
17. **Deployment with documented rationale:** accounts of what a one-site, one-control comparison can and can't support, so our conclusions don't overreach the data.

We plan on ensuring that we’re able to meet our project purpose and design focus by following our project plan, tracked as a board on Google Sheets, and meeting once a week to touch-base on task progression. To devise our project plan, we started by breaking down our project into key steps, from which we classified specific workstreams. These workstreams help us track key project milestones, and are grouped as follows: Interviews, CAD design (box), PCB design (all electronic components), web app design, breadboarding, Arduino code writing, testing on breadboard, assembly (soldering components to PCB \+ putting into box \+ assembling), final prototype testing, and the demo video.   
These workstream groupings ensure that multiple project topics are able to be completed in parallel by workstream pairs (e.g. one pair working on CAD while another works on PCB design). We have planned and set deadlines for workstream milestones as per chronological order and dependencies \-- e.g. testing the first breadboard with Arduino code early on, prior to PCB design, to ensure that the code is working and feasible. This plan is as follows (full project taskboard found [here](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1vhLOYaInnmqE-ZmZ9ZWyuajkHLCShk9RheqDHhVeM-E/edit?gid=663629714#gid=663629714)):
